references.title: Utilizing Student Time Series Behaviour in Learning Management Systems for Early Prediction of Course Performance

extracted.Target: Good vs Poor performance (Poor = C+ or below; Good = above B-)
extracted.Students: Education course: 141 students in semester 1 and 527 students in semester 2; Follow-up biology course: 290 students in semester 1 and 311 students in semester 2
extracted.Courses: 1 mandatory undergraduate education course; follow-up in 1 mandatory introductory biology course
extracted.Features:
Main deep-learning input: daily click frequencies over the first 28, 42, 56, or 70 days. 

For conventional ML baselines, 21 aggregated LMS features were extracted.
extracted.Moment of Prediction: Early prediction at multiple time points: first 28, 42, 56, and 70 days of the course
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Data from semester 2 of the education course were split into training/validation (72%, n = 407) and test (28%, n = 120); semester 1 was used as an external/generalizability test. Class imbalance was handled with SMOTE on training data because very few students actually failed. Missing values were imputed with kNN imputation. Aggregated ML features were standardized (mean 0, SD 1). Feature selection for conventional ML used RFE with Random Forest, resulting in 7 selected features.
extracted.Models: LSTM,MLP, Logistic Regression, Naïve Bayes, Gradient Boosting Machine, SVM, Decision Tree, kNN, Random Forest
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model:
Main study (education course): {Validation AUC: 75.2% – LSTM with first 56 days; Test AUC Semester 1: 68.2% – LSTM with first 42 days; Test AUC Semester 2: 68.2% – LSTM with first 42 days}. 

Best conventional ML baseline reached 66.2% AUC (Naïve Bayes with selected features, validation; also 66.2% on Semester 2 test for selected features). 

Follow-up biology course: LSTM achieved up to 83.0% validation AUC and about 78.7% test AUC on semester 1.
extracted.Comments: The paper’s main contribution is methodological: showing that LSTM on simple daily click-frequency time series can outperform conventional ML models using engineered aggregated LMS features, while also showing somewhat stronger generalizability across semesters and course types. The most influential baseline features were mostly frequency-related rather than time-related, supporting the choice of click frequencies as the core representation. A follow-up study in a biology course further supports generalizability.
extracted.Limitations:
Sample sizes are still relatively small for deep learning. 

The definition of “at-risk” is somewhat arbitrary because actual failures were rare, so students with C+ or below were treated as poor performers. 

Class imbalance required SMOTE, which may introduce bias. 

Generalizability across semesters was only moderate, and the approach was tested in only two institutional course contexts.

annotations.text: Most attempts that look to use time-series representations to predict student performance seem to underwhelm. Thus, the authors propose a method to use daily-click counts to train a LSTM model to predict end-of-course performance. Comparison shows that the approach outperforms traditional approaches.
